---
title: "Try It Yourself"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    code-annotations: hover
    code-fold: true
    code-fold-label: "Show Code"
    css: styles.css
editor: visual
---

# 🧪 Try It Yourself: Test a Hypothesis

Now it’s your turn to apply what you’ve learned!

In the activities below, you’ll explore real clinical datasets and run statistical tests to answer research questions — just like a real analyst or health researcher.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 📦 Dataset 1: `strep_tb`

This dataset comes from a 1948 clinical trial testing **streptomycin** for treating tuberculosis. It includes:

- **Treatment group**:
  - `control`: standard bed rest\
  - `streptomycin`: bed rest + new antibiotic treatment
- **Outcome variables**:
  - `rad_num`: Radiologic score (1–6, based on X-ray damage)\
  - `improved`: TRUE if `rad_num` ≥ 5

### 🧾 Other useful variables

| Variable  | Description                              |
|-----------|------------------------------------------|
| `age`     | Patient age                              |
| `sex`     | Patient sex (male/female)                |
| `arm`     | Treatment group (Streptomycin / Control) |
| `outcome` | Clinical outcome based on radiology      |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 🧪 t-Test: Compare Group Means {.callout-tip title="① t-Test: Compare Group Means"}

> **Research Question:**\
> Do radiologic scores differ between the treatment and control groups?

This test compares **numeric outcomes** between two independent groups.

▶️ [**Try It Yourself: t-Test**](https://jalfaro.shinyapps.io/try-it-yourself-t-test/){.btn .btn-outline-pink}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 🔲 Chi-square: Categorical Association {.callout-warning title="② Chi-square: Categorical Association"}

> **Research Question:**\
> Is there a relationship between treatment group and improvement outcome?

This test examines the association between **two categorical variables**.

▶️ [**Try It Yourself: Chi-square**](https://jalfaro.shinyapps.io/try-it-yourself-chi-square/){.btn .btn-outline-pink}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 📦 Dataset 2: `smartpill`

This dataset comes from a modern study using the **SmartPill capsule** to measure digestion time through the gastrointestinal tract.

- **Participants**:
  - 87 healthy adults\
  - 22 critically ill patients\
- **Key measures**:
  - `GE.Time`: Gastric emptying time (hrs)\
  - `WG.Time`: Whole gut transit time (hrs)

### 🧾 Other useful variables

| Variable | Description                     |
|----------|---------------------------------|
| `Group`  | 1 = Healthy, 0 = Critically ill |
| `Gender` | 0 = Female, 1 = Male            |
| `Age`    | Age in years                    |
| `Weight` | Weight in kilograms             |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 🔗 Correlation: Two Numeric Variables {.callout-note title="③ Correlation: Two Numeric Variables"}

> **Research Question:**\
> Do people who take longer to empty their stomach also take longer to digest food overall?

This test checks the **strength and direction of association** between two numeric variables.

▶️ [**Try It Yourself: Correlation**](https://jalfaro.shinyapps.io/try-it-yourself-correlation/){.btn .btn-outline-pink}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## ✅ Self-Assessment: What Have You Learned?

Use this checklist to reflect on your progress.

::: checklist-box
### 🧪 t-Test

- [ ] I know when to use a t-test\
- [ ] I can compare the means of two groups\
- [ ] I can interpret the *t*-statistic, *p*-value\
- [ ] I can report t-test results using correct notation

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 🔲 Chi-square Test

- [ ] I can summarise categorical outcomes in a contingency table\
- [ ] I understand how to run a Chi-square test of independence\
- [ ] I know what a significant association tells us about two variables\
- [ ] I can report the test statistic, degrees of freedom, and *p*-value

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 🔗 Correlation

- [ ] I understand when to use Pearson’s correlation\
- [ ] I can describe the **direction**, **strength**, and **significance** of *r*\
- [ ] I know that correlation ≠ causation\
- [ ] I can interpret *r* and *p*-value
:::
